summary_text:
Chief Complaint
To ensure her kidneys are looking good right now and to schedule a kidney ultrasound.

History of Present Illness
The patient's twin, Katherine, was born prematurely at 34 and 3 weeks and weighed 3 pounds 8 ounces and 3 pounds 13 ounces. She experienced feeding difficulties, including pauses in breathing during feeding, and was subsequently put on a cow's milk-free diet. She has been diagnosed with Smith-Magenis syndrome, which is associated with congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract, and has experienced reflex issues. She has received extensive therapy, including feeding, occupational, and physical therapy, and has been seen by an allergist, neurologist, and ophthalmologist. The patient reports that she has had many therapies and that her family has seen many specialists. She is currently taking ripple pea protein milk and water. She is now crawling and trying to take steps. She has a lot of wet diapers, around 8 to 10 per day, and drinks a lot. She is 22 months old and is approximately the same size as her twin, who is a girl.

Findings
Physical exam reveals a well-nourished female who is slightly fussy when examined. Her eyes appear small, and she exhibits mild hypotonia of the lower extremities. External genitalia are normal. The doctor listened to her heart and lungs and examined her ears.

Assessment
Katherine is a 22-month-old former 34 and 3-week-old, twin with Smith-Magenis syndrome. She has congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract, and may require further evaluation.

Plan
We will obtain the screening of the kidneys by ultrasound today. If there are abnormalities on the kidney ultrasound, we will determine next steps and future follow-up. The family lives in Dallas, Georgia, so her follow-up should be at the town center location. I will call you with the results. If it's normal, you won't need to see me again, but if it's abnormal, you can see me in Kennesaw. The nurse will be in to have you complete some paperwork and give you instructions for the ultrasound.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Renal screening tests.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Katherine Lopez is a 22-month-old female, born at 34 weeks +3 days gestation, diagnosed with the rare Smith-Magenis syndrome and associated global developmental delays. She was referred to nephrology by genetics who recommended renal screening tests. At birth, the patient weighed 4 pounds 8 ounces, exhibited difficulties during eating where she would stop breathing, and challenges regulating her body temperature; determined to be typical issues associated with premature birth. The patient has seen many specialists, including GI for severe reflux, a dietitian who recommended discontinuing consumption of cow’s milk, and a neurologist who diagnosed her with Smith-Magenis syndrome. Additionally, she has been seen by a geneticist, orthopedist, ophthalmologist, and audiologist. The patient is not ambulatory, did not sit up unassisted until 1 years old, and is currently crawling and attempting to take steps. She will be starting speech therapy in the next few weeks, and currently she is in occupational, feeding, and physical therapy. The geneticist informed the patient's mother that patients with Smith-Magenis syndrome usually have kidney issues where “the kidneys fuse into one” and issues with their legs.

The patient has approximately 8 to 10 wet diapers in a 24-hour period. Her mother notes that she is doing better with drinking fluids and she urinates “a lot”. The patient drinks Ripple Pea Protein milk instead of cow’s milk, water and water mixed with a small amount of juice. Her twin sister is approximately the same size, and their weight is essentially the same, she is healthy, and the mom denies any known health concerns or diagnoses. The mom states that Katherine is shorter and seems chubbier related to her syndrome. She also has a 9-year-old brother who is diagnosed with autism.

BIRTH HISTORY

Twin gestation. Birth weight 4 pounds 8 ounces.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Smith-Magenis syndrome.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient accompanied to appointment by her mother.
Sibling: 9-year-old brother and twin sister.

FAMILY HISTORY

Brother: 9 years old, positive for autism.
Twin sister: absence of Smith-Magenis syndrome, no known health conditions.
No known family history of genetic conditions, kidney failure, dialysis, or kidney transplant.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Well-nourished female, slightly fussy when examined.

Eyes
Presences of microphthalmia.

Genitourinary
Normal external female genitalia.

Musculoskeletal
Extremities: Presence of mild hypotonia of lower extremities and the arms.

ASSESSMENT

• Smith-Magenis syndrome.
Katherine is a 22-month-old former 34+3-week-old twin with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
Several organ systems can be affected by this chromosomal deletion syndrome and congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract have been reported in the literature.

PLAN

We will obtain a kidney ultrasound screening today. If there are abnormalities on the kidney ultrasound, we will determine the next steps and future follow-up. The family lives in Dallas, Georgia, so her follow-up should be at the Town Center location.

INSTRUCTIONS

Complete ultrasound today. I will call patient with results and possible next steps.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's twin, Katherine, was born prematurely at 34 and 3 weeks and weighed 3 pounds 8 ounces and 3 pounds 13 ounces.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence is written as if the twin is the subject of the note rather than the patient, creating confusion about who the HPI is describing. However, it is background history relevant to the visit and does not fundamentally misplace exam, plan, or diagnostic reasoning content.

section_miscategorizations.text: She has been diagnosed with Smith-Magenis syndrome, which is associated with congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract, and has experienced reflex issues.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The diagnosis of Smith-Magenis syndrome and associated congenital anomalies represents an established problem list/diagnostic impression, which typically belongs in the Assessment. Its presence in HPI is acceptable as background but is organizationally suboptimal.

section_miscategorizations.text: Katherine is a 22-month-old former 34 and 3-week-old, twin with Smith-Magenis syndrome.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is largely demographic and perinatal history that would usually be summarized in the HPI. In Assessment it is somewhat redundant, but it does not misrepresent findings or plans.

section_miscategorizations.text: She has congenital anomalies of the kidney and urinary tract, and may require further evaluation.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The congenital anomalies are described as pre-existing history rather than a new synthesized impression from this visit. While acceptable in Assessment as part of the problem list, it is more strictly historical information.
